metric_value: ~30% per year
notes: IT deflation theme: servers replacing older boxes at same or lower prices with 30% more processing power per year.

metric_name: Software licensing risk from lower processor counts
metric_value: Significant; fewer processors per server reducing per-processor license revenue
notes: Server consolidation means today's servers have fewer processors than predecessors, harming per-processor software license revenue.

metric_value: Skyrocketing due to compliance, replication, PC data proliferation
notes: Storage budgets rising; compliance, real-time replication, and personal data proliferation driving growth; ILM emerging as response.

metric_value: Early/emerging; new thinking about tiered storage pools
notes: Opening doors to new lower-cost storage architectures; not all data bytes have equal value to enterprise.

metric_value: Increasing in retail automation, CRM, analytics
notes: Empowered consumer driving IT investments in retail: online info, kiosks, self-checkout, loyalty programs backed by decision support.

metric_value: ~1/3 consider themselves IT-savvy; another 1/3 at least competent
notes: Consumer IT sophistication megachange enabling empowered buyers using Internet for price research.

metric_value: CIOs seeing integration costs favoring single-vendor suites
notes: End of best-of-breed era: CIOs view multi-vendor integration as more expensive; CFOs prefer pre-integrated applications; technology firm consolidation accelerating.

metric_value: Executive committee now controls budget; wants high-ROI projects implementable in weeks not months
notes: Value in IT is relative: great ROI only funds projects addressing executive's top 3-5 pain points. Speed of implementation now critical.

metric_value: Large firms own dozens of redundant/overlapping software products; multi-million dollar unused purchases
notes: IT shopping addiction: CIOs/CFOs rationalizing redundant hardware/software; remote data centers being consolidated.

metric_value: RFID tags, automated replenishment, enhanced parcel tracking driven by modern consumer
notes: Dell and Wal-Mart cited as channel masters whose success driven by empowered modern consumer.

metric_value: Real-time click tracking replacing survey/Nielsen panel methods
notes: Anonymous web click data provides better behavioral insights than surveys; privacy concern emerging but muted.

metric_value: ~30% of enterprise computers are laptops; few takers for dramatic increase
notes: Survey of 750,000 business computer buyers; road warriors under 10% of enterprise users; corridor cruisers need WiFi to justify laptop investment.

metric_value: Security fears, high support cost, high initial cost cited by executives
notes: Huge capital invested in wireless infrastructure (hotspots) will be hard to profit without more enterprise laptop buyers.

metric_value: Extended value chains vastly complicating enterprise management; partner autonomy conflicts
notes: Partner-keeper technologies needed; risk of large value-chain integration projects if adoption too slow.

metric_value: Several hundred thousand to several million dollars per year per affected business
notes: Some firms reverting to private status to avoid SOX compliance costs; Basel II also cited. Affects B2B transactions and business processes.

metric_name: IT deflation impact on commodity services
metric_value: Commodity prices and cradle-to-grave services incompatible business models
notes: IT vendors must find profits somewhere if pricing commoditized; pre/post-sales services at risk under pure commodity pricing.

metric_value: Consumers expect purchase via store, phone, web, PDA; mass customization on demand
notes: Modern consumer forces adoption of numerous technologies at every step of manufacturing, distribution, and sales.

metric_value: Uncertain; hotspot investments hard to profit without corporate laptop adoption growth
notes: Prediction: 2004 may see breakthrough in enterprise laptop buying enabling new productivity round.

metric_value: [UNVERIFIED]
notes: WiFi hotspot revenue and subscriber adoption were revised downward by analysts in 2004. InStat/MDR estimated only $28M in US hotspot revenue in 2003, well below forecasts. Wall Street Journal noted adoption hadn't met predictions. Small WISP operators largely failed. Pricing remained high ($25-75/month) creating a 'chicken and egg' problem. The eventual hotspot proliferation came through free WiFi at venues (Starbucks moved from T-Mobile to AT&T in 2008, then to free WiFi), not through the paid subscription model envisioned. T-Mobile Hotspot (Starbucks) did not achieve broad profitability as a standalone business; Starbucks dropped T-Mobile for AT&T in 2008 and ultimately moved to free WiFi.

metric_name: SOX compliance cost impact on public companies
metric_value: Some firms may revert to private status due to compliance costs
notes: Prediction that SOX costs are 'corrosive to earnings'; some public companies may go private.

metric_value: [UNVERIFIED]
notes: SOX Section 404 compliance costs significantly exceeded initial estimates. AMR Research (2005) found companies would spend nearly $15.5B on compliance-related activities in 2005, with 28% of SOX spending on technology. Average compliance costs for smaller companies reached ~$1.8M per $1B in sales. Audit fees increased by 43% for smaller firms and 23% for larger firms post-SOX (Berkeley study). SOX drove a dramatic increase in IT outsourcing to Indian IT vendors (Wall St. Journal 2005). An astounding 93% of CIOs were initially unaware of their IT control assessment responsibilities under SOX (survey). Compliance spending grew 50% in technology between 2004 and 2005. The overall impact was substantial, expensive, and transformative for IT governance and compliance tech spending.
